topic: Someone receives an apology letter years after the incident
raw_output:
[story 1]

The thick, cream-colored envelope landed on my cluttered desk with a soft, definitive thud. I didn't even look at the address first; I just stared at the stark, formal script of the return address. It was from Mark. Years. Years had passed since that disastrous night, the one that had left a permanent stain on everything. My jaw tightened so hard I could feel the muscle twitching beneath my skin. I picked up the letter, the paper feeling brittle and strangely cold under my fingertips. I unfolded it slowly, the sound of the tearing paper echoing loudly in the otherwise silent office. "I am truly sorry," the first sentence read. A practiced, polished apology. I let out a short, sharp breath, the air catching painfully in my chest. I crumpled the paper once, then smoothed it out again, just to see the neat, looping handwriting that had once seemed so careless. I tossed it onto the pile of unopened mail, a gesture of profound dismissal.

[story 2]

I found the letter tucked inside a forgotten book at the back of the attic, a relic from a summer I thought I’d buried. When I finally opened it, the familiar wave of irritation washed over me, hot and prickly. It was from David. The words were flowery, full of self-flagellation and vague promises of future amends. I read it twice, my eyes scanning the text as if searching for a hidden clause that would invalidate the entire document. My fingers drummed a rapid, uneven rhythm on the wooden floorboards. I stood up abruptly, pacing the small space between the dusty trunks. I stopped by the window, staring out at the indifferent cityscape, feeling a profound sense of weariness settle over my shoulders. I let out a slow, deliberate sigh, the sound heavy with accumulated frustration, before finally dropping the letter onto the floor with a soft, almost resentful thud.

[story 3]

The notification pinged on my phone, a mundane sound that instantly morphed into a jolt of unwelcome energy. It was an email, decades old, from Sarah. I clicked it open, bracing myself for the usual emotional ambush. Instead, there was just the apology. It was long, detailed, and meticulously worded, outlining every perceived wrong, every regret. Reading it felt like wading through thick, stagnant water. My hands started shaking slightly; I had to grip the edge of the desk to keep them steady. I reread the final paragraph, the one where she expressed remorse for the impact of her actions. I closed my eyes, pinching the bridge of my nose until it ached. I opened them, fixed her name on the screen, and without a word, I deleted the message, the screen going dark in response to my sudden, sharp need for silence.
